speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] Getting up to speed. Go ahead and slate it, John.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Suse Rosenstock, previous name Herz, on January 19, 1996 in San Diego, California. My name is John Kent, and we're doing this in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell your full name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My full name, Suse Margot Rosenstock, S-U-S-E M-A-R-G-O-T R-O-S-E-N-S-T-O-C-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name was Herz, H-E-R-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date and current age?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date is May 6, 1931. So, at present, I am 64 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country where you were born, and spell it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Worms on the Rhine in Germany, W-O-R-M-S. And it's on the river Rhine, and Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the names of your, your mother and father and siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Albert, Albert Herz. My mother was Flora Herz. And I have one sister, Edith Herz, now Pagelson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's already done an interview also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about your family? What was your mother or father like? What did they do in the hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a hardware store in the town of Worms. My father was a businessman. My mother worked in the store with my father. And we had an apprentice whose name happens also to be Herz, but no relative, who worked for us in the store. There was a woman in the office. We sold farm implements also to the farmers that were in the surrounding area in those days. You sold household items, but as I said, also farm implements, axes, and the things that you need on a farm. My father would go and deliver these things. Sometimes I would go with him. So I got to meet some of these farmers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the standard of living like with this kind of profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: The standard of living was a good one. We were middle class people. We had a lovely apartment right near the store. It was across the courtyard. And we were-- you know, my parents would play cards with friends. And we had art, music in the house. My father was always afraid that I wouldn't be able to carry a tune because he couldn't. And my mother worked very hard at fostering that so that we would also always appreciate music and art. And of course, it was a good standard of living.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about your father's views on political things and Jewish issues and community values?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Jewish issues, of course, we belonged to a synagogue. We were members, active members. It was-- it's the oldest synagogue in Germany, the synagogue in Worms. It has the Rashi room attached to it where Rashi taught. It had its own mikveh. I guess I was really too young as far as knowing politics or anything else. My father, of course, I heard him say all the time, I fought in the First World War. And therefore, nothing's going to happen to me. But other than that, politics and things like that were not discussed in families as openly as they are today.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about your mother? What kind of a person was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a strong woman, a woman of, of strength, of character, of, of opinions. If something had to be done, it got done. She was-- my parents, I suppose, were both strict in upbringing. Education was a, a big thing in our family. If we didn't bring home the best marks, not so much as punishment, but we were berated for it, that's for sure. So, you know, these things were very, very important in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a young person were you? How would you describe yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a normal child wanting to always do what my older sister did. She's four and a half years older than I. And, of course, in those years, that was a lot of difference. She had a lot of different privileges than I did, and I didn't like that. I wanted to be just like her. I was just a normal girl who got into scrapes, who got, who got dirty when I wasn't supposed to. A friendly child, I know that. We had lots of friends. I also enjoyed learning. And I enjoyed getting good marks in school because it pleased my parents and it pleased me. So it was, it was fun doing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way were you and your family Jewish, in addition to going to a synagogue? What values did your family have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the values were always there. We celebrated all the holidays. And of course, particularly, we were a close family. We celebrated with aunts and uncles and grandparents. We went to [NON-ENGLISH] where my grandparents lived. And we spent wonderful holidays there, particularly Passover. I mean, how can you forget the stacks and stacks of matzahs that disappeared. And you, and you wondered where. Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashanah, where grandmother baked huge plum cakes and crumb cakes. Everything was done before you even got up in the morning. You wondered whether she'd been be doing this all night long. We were taught prayers. We were taught what the holidays were about, as I said, the celebration and the meaning of them, how to give a Seder. [NON-ENGLISH] was sung after meals in the house. And this was all learned in school because by the time I went to school, we were not allowed to go to a public school, but to a Jewish parochial school. So our education was wonderful. It was both Jewish and secular. We had great teachers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, even though you were young, what do you remember of how your family regarded the non-Jewish people in the community? Were you fairly assimilated, or was there a distinct line?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was assimilation. We had a woman who took care of us. Today, she would be called, I guess, a daycare person. And she was not Jewish. They were-- had communist leanings, I believe. But we were certainly on very good terms with them. As a matter of fact, I remember very fondly going on Sunday mornings during the season that cherries were ripe. We were-- we would go to her house and pick cherries. They were the white cherries, which were particularly delicious. And we just had a wonderful time doing it. Yeah, there was, there was interaction with non-Jews, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first become aware that there was some kind of danger or antagonism towards Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess it was when we were going to school, not being able to interact or play with the non-Jewish children in the area, where the parents would grab the kids and bring-- take them in, or when we were chased home from school and had stones thrown at us. And we would-- I know I would run particularly-- or try and run particularly fast to get away. And if I would trip and fall, I would end up coming home with a bump on the front of the head and a bump in the back from the-- from the stones, you know, being hurt. So that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what they said to you while they were chasing you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They used all the epithets that there were normally, you know. Dirty Jew and, sort of, we don't want you here type of things. Specifically, I don't think I, you know, know. But it was the epithets that are used all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do after that? Did you try to get an explanation, or what? From your parents, from teachers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you, you, you-- yeah, of course. And what's the-- what was the explanation? Unfortunately, you're Jewish, and they don't want anything to do with us. Trying to go into the stores and buying things, food or other articles, was also a difficult thing. And you realized there and then that you were persona non grata. They didn't want to sell to you. And the same happened, of course, the boycotting of our own store, where they would come at night and write 'Dirty Jew. Don't buy here' in-- in big red letters in front of the store that my father, in the middle of the night, then would go out and scrub off. So it was, it was very evident. There wasn't-- childhood sort of melted away. You grew up. You-- you realized that you, that you had to take responsibility, I guess. And that was something also that came from our, from my parents, from my mother and my father, that things were serious. It wasn't all fun and games anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your memory of the Crystal Night? You were about five years old then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: Seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was seven. Crystal Night is something that's etched in my memory and, of course, will be forever. My father had been saying Kaddish for my grandparents from my mother's parents who had died and ran home quickly to tell us that there would be no school that morning. That was November 10th that the synagogue was on fire. And my mother was in the store with my father at the time. And she ran upstairs and gathered my sister and me, and we went to hide in the attic.

speaker: interviewer
text: A terrifying experience because it was the shattering of glass. It was the terror of not knowing what was going to happen to us. And we were all crouching together, the three of us, not being able to move. And at seven and a half years old, things like going to the bathroom, you know, or just trying to keep still. My mother wanted to go and save what she could. And here she was with two little girls, what was she going to do? And she stayed with us. Cold, basically hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, after a long time, coming downstairs and viewing what had been done to our house by other people, the carnage, the smells. There was still smells that remain in your nostrils somehow forever. A cupboard was in the kitchen, was completely smashed. And in the cupboard had been herring and a substance, it's called maggi, M-A-G-G-I, which is similar to a soy sauce. And those two smells mingled together. Still, I can still remember them. It's a long time to remember that kind of thing. But it's strange. It happens. Seeing all the glass, all your belongings on the street, having to gather them up. And then, of course, watching my father being taken away, not knowing where. And there we were in this mess, my mother with two little girls. But again, my mother's tremendous strength. It was the same as we say today, you know, you put one foot in front of the other and you keep on going. And that's exactly what she did. She first wanted to find out, of course, what they had done with her husband, my father. And it was not until a day or so later, after me hanging onto her skirts and going to every office in the town to find out what had happened to the men, that you find out that he had been taken to Buchenwald. And I did an awful lot of crying. And I was then sent to my mother's sister in Frankfurt by myself. My mother could not go with me. But she bought a ticket, and fortunately, I was allowed on the train. And I held onto that ticket. And my aunt met me in Frankfurt and picked me up. She had two little children. And I stayed with her for a couple of weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And something strange I remember about that is that, you know, we were not allowed into movies or certain places, Jews are not allowed. But somehow, I remember that that was a year that came out. And I remember seeing And I was seven and a half. And it's a vivid memory. And I do remember now. How I got into that movie house, I don't know how my aunt got me into it. But I remember seeing on the screen. Funny memory, right? So-- How long was your father taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, I guess, was, was probably around two months or so. And by the time he came back, he was-- he didn't even look like our father. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was he different?

speaker: interviewee
text: The weight he'd lost, and the stress and the strain and the dirt. I mean, just the way that he looked, he-- word was gotten to us that he was coming home. And we had this big gate that closed off the-- that you had to go into-- actually, the car was driven into next to the store. And we waited for his familiar knock that night that he came home. Sort of the middle of the night, I remember standing behind that gate with my mother and my sister. That was something that happened in families too so that you would know if it was a familiar knock or if it was an enemy knock, shall we say, for someone to come in. Each family sort of adopted a code of their own. And we had one too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did your parents start to talk about at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I know that my mother had written letters to people in the States and wherever to see if they could help us. But of course, it was not forthcoming. And my mother bought vegetables from a woman at the market. And the woman warned my mother and said, you know, Mrs. Herz, take your family and go. And my mother sort of laughed and said, where am I going to go when no one wants us. And so we, we didn't. And once my father was home, my mother had an aunt in the town of Duisburg who offered her a job as a housekeeper. And of course, she told them that she would have to take-- bring me a long because I was only seven and a half years old. And what would happen is she would-- they would leave my sister to help my father to cook, and wash, and do all that sort of thing, and help him with the cleaning up there. And I went with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was at that point in time that, somehow or other, and that I do not-- I don't know the exact circumstances, that mother found out about Kindertransport. And she called my father and said, we're sending Suse. And my father objected, she's not going to be with Jews. How do we know where she's going? And so on and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother, plain and simply, sort of said to him, you know, hey, you didn't register us at the embassy. Because that had been talked about, that my father please go, before Buchenwald already, before Kristallnacht, to register us at the American embassy to get a number, at least, to see if we could get out. And he said, no, I fought for them in the First World War. They're not going to do anything to me. And my mother said, you know, don't be so sure. Now, of course, here we go again. And my mother, again, the strong lady-- and I've heard of this many times before where the women were the ones that pushed for emigration. It's a difficult decision for anybody. You know, I mean, here were four people. It's not easy to emigrate to a country where you don't know the language. But my mother insisted and said, let's at least save one member of the family, because things are not going to be-- get any better.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so when a very short time, it had to be within a couple of weeks, that I was on my way. Now, how was I on my way? Possibly-- first of all, there had to be an exchange of money. How much, I cannot tell you. But of course, Kindertransport was an agreement between Germany and Britain, that Britain would take in 10,000 unaccompanied children.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a suitcase packed in our house, which was actually for my sister Edith. My sister Edith was supposed to have come to Cincinnati, Ohio. There was a family who was going to take her in. But we never heard. Well, of course, the State Department in the United States was isolationist at that time and canceled the acceptance of 20,000 children from Germany to America. And we have reason to believe that my sister Edith was one of those. So that suitcase had been packed for Edith to come to America. And now, it became my suitcase. And it was going to England with me. The clothes in it were clothes that had come from a department store that had been owned by my mother's aunt and uncle that had been taken over by the Nazis. But before that was done, they managed to take some stuff out. And clothes, as I said, were given to us and also to my cousins, both in the town in Frankfurt and then in the town of [NON-ENGLISH] in Holland. And so on July 25, 1939, I left for England. My mother and I left on a train from Duisburg to go to Worms at 5 o'clock in the morning. There I was-- we were to meet at the station, my father and my sister, to say goodbye. Only one parent, one guardian-- parent, guardian, whatever-- was allowed to go with you to the transport train. We reached Worms, and there-- as I say, I can-- I think I can see it as clear as it had happened yesterday-- were my father and my sister at the railway station. And we said goodbye. And then, we, my mother and I, went further to the town of Mainz. Now, there was another thing. We had not been able to get Dramamine for the sea journey for me. No one would sell it to us. So you know, there was another thing of, of, of the anti-Semitism, of the dirty Jew. My mother did buy me a pen. I remember distinctly it was a pelican pen. And it was encased in a little leather tube case [INAUDIBLE] and put that into my bag. And the train pulled in at Mainz. That was my transport train. There probably were 400 or 500 on that train.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said this Kindertransport wasn't only for Jewish kids, it was for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Only for Jewish kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, only?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only for Jewish kids from the age of babies to 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The train pulled in, and that's the first time that I saw my mother flinch. The first two cars of my train were babies in hammocks. There were people there. There were women, apparently. There was somebody in a white uniform. I don't know who or what they were, I suppose watching over these. But we do-- I do remember seeing those first two railway cars with hammocks and babies in them. My mother saw that, and that was the first time in all those horrible months that I saw her flinch. I mean, she was going to put her daughter onto that train. And of course, all the usual admonitions. But the last thing that she said to me was-- one of the last, I guess, things was, be a good girl. Whoever you're going to go to, help in the household. And just be a good girl. Nothing really saying to me, I'll see you again soon or whatever. It was my behavior, that I do things that are right. This is the way that I was brought up. You do the proper thing all the time. And so I got onto the train all by myself with a big suitcase-- she couldn't even help me get that up-- and sat in a compartment with probably nine others. We were probably about five people, you know, facing one another, five and five. And that's the way I sat and remained. It was a terrifying experience. I just don't remember a thing about it.
